plainMeaning: It is said that in the realm of the Nāgakumāra gods there are eighty-four lakh Jina-temples, and by bowing to them all I become firmly settled in right faith.
recitation: nāgakumāra māṁhe kahyā, tihāṁ lākha corāśī | etā jinahara tihāṁ namuṁ, thāuṁ samakitavāsī ||

wordByWord.gloss: the Nāgakumāra ('serpent-prince') gods — the second of the ten Bhavanapati classes
wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit nāga ('serpent') + kumāra ('youth, prince'); a residential (bhavanavāsin) deva-class who appear ever-youthful and princely.
wordByWord.notes: Their indra-lords are Dharaṇa and Bhūtānanda; their realm lies in the upper Ratnaprabhā.

wordByWord.gloss: among, within, in the midst of
wordByWord.etymology: Old Gujarati / Apabhraṁśa locative postposition, from Sanskrit madhye ('in the middle of').
wordByWord.notes: Locates the count inside the Nāgakumāra world.

wordByWord.gloss: are said, are told (by the scriptures/cosmographers)
wordByWord.etymology: Past participle of Gujarati kahevuṁ, from Sanskrit kathita ('spoken, declared').
wordByWord.notes: Impersonal 'it is said', flagging the figure as received tradition, not the poet's own claim.

wordByWord.etymology: Old Gujarati demonstrative adverb of place, from Prakrit tahiṁ / Sanskrit tatra.
wordByWord.notes: First of two occurrences in this verse; both point back to the Nāgakumāra world.
Pairs with corāśī to give the total.

wordByWord.etymology: From Sanskrit caturaśīti ('84'), via Prakrit cõrāsī.
wordByWord.notes: Eighty-four lakh = 8,400,000 — the traditional tally of śāśvata Jina-temples in the Nāgakumāra realm.

wordByWord.etymology: Old Gujarati quantitative demonstrative (etlā / etā), from Prakrit ettiya / Sanskrit etāvat ('this much').
wordByWord.notes: Gathers the eighty-four lakh temples just counted into a single object of homage.

wordByWord.etymology: jina ('Conqueror') + hara/-ghar, vernacular for ghara ('house') = 'house of the Jina'; equivalent to Sanskrit jinālaya / jina-bhavana.
wordByWord.notes: Here the eternal (śāśvata) temples of the lower world.

wordByWord.etymology: As above, from Prakrit tahiṁ / Sanskrit tatra.
wordByWord.notes: Repetition is emphatic — the worshipper directs his bow toward that distant realm.

wordByWord.etymology: First-person present of Gujarati namavuṁ, from Sanskrit √nam ('to bow').
wordByWord.notes: The core caitya-vandana act, performed mentally toward unreachable temples.

wordByWord.etymology: First-person present of Gujarati thavuṁ ('to become'), from Prakrit thā- / Sanskrit √sthā ('to stand, to be').
wordByWord.notes: States the fruit that follows from the bowing.

wordByWord.etymology: samakita (vernacular for Sanskrit samyaktva, 'right faith') + vāsī ('one who dwells', from √vas, 'to reside').
wordByWord.notes: 'One whose abode is samyaktva' — the spiritual settledness gained by the act of homage.

literalTranslation: Among the Nāgakumāra (gods) it is said [there are] eighty-four lakh [Jina-temples] there; to these many Jina-temples there I bow, [and] I become a dweller in right faith.
idiomaticTranslation: In the world of the Nāgakumāra gods, the scriptures declare, there stand eighty-four lakh Jina-temples; to every one of those shrines I now bow — and so I make right faith my very dwelling-place.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This couplet is the second bead in the lower-world rosary the poet is telling: the verse before bowed in the Asurakumāra realm (sixty-four lakh shrines), and the verse after will move on to the Suvarṇakumāra (seventy-two lakh) and Vāyukumāra (ninety-six lakh). Here the Nāgakumāra, second of the ten residential (bhavanapati) deva-classes, contribute their eighty-four lakh jinhar. The structure is deliberately formulaic — name a class, cite its temple-count 'as it is said' (kahyā), and answer the staggering number with a single small verb of devotion (namuṁ) — and the formula is the point: the worshipper cannot visit even one of these subterranean shrines, yet the bow reaches them all. What distinguishes this verse from its neighbours is its closing line. Where the previous couplet ended in the gesture of homage (juhāra), this one names the fruit of homage: thāuṁ samakitavāsī, 'I become one who dwells in right faith.' The two finite first-person verbs, namuṁ … thāuṁ, bind cause to effect in a single breath — bowing is not merely expressed but is shown to do something to the reciter, settling him into samyaktva. The doubled tihāṁ ('there … there') keeps the gaze fixed on a realm the eye can never reach, so that the whole act is interior: a census recited becomes a faith inhabited.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse rests on the Jaina teaching of śāśvata (eternal, uncreated) Jina-temples standing in the deva-realms, perpetually worshipped by the gods — here the eighty-four-lakh tally assigned to the Nāgakumāra in the Śvetāmbara cosmographic reckoning. To bow to images and temples one will never physically see is itself an exercise of samyaktva, which Hemacandra defines as faith in the right deva, right guru and right dharma: the reciter affirms the Jina's presence on the strength of scripture ('kahyā') rather than sight. The closing samakitavāsī thus states the verse's specific doctrinal yield — universe-wide reverence is the means by which right faith is not merely held but made one's permanent abode. The vast number is a devotional instrument meant to enlarge faith, not a sum to be audited.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: At dawn the reciter, unable to reach a single Nāgakumāra shrine, bows to all eighty-four lakh in the mind — training the habit of revering the Jina beyond what the senses can verify, and consciously taking the resolve 'thāuṁ samakitavāsī': let this act seat me a little more firmly in right faith today.

crossReferences.text: Tattvārthasūtra 4.10–4.11 (the ten classes of bhavanavāsin devas, incl. Nāgakumāra under Dharaṇa and Bhūtānanda)
crossReferences.source: Umāsvāti; trans. in Encyclopedia of Jainism / Tattvārtha Sūtra ch. 4

crossReferences.text: Saṃgrahaṇīratna (Bṛhat-saṃgrahaṇī) — Śvetāmbara cosmography of bhavana and śāśvata-jinālaya counts
Triṣaṣṭiśalākāpuruṣacaritra (treatment of samyaktva as faith in deva-guru-dharma)
crossReferences.source: Hemacandra; trans. Helen M. Johnson, Gaekwad Oriental Series

sources.title: Nāgakumāra — definitions (Jain cosmology; Tattvārthasūtra 4.10; Cappeller & Monier-Williams Sanskrit lexica)
sources.consultedFor: Identity of the Nāgakumāra as the serpent-prince class of bhavanavāsin devas, their lords Dharaṇa and Bhūtānanda, and the Sanskrit etymology nāga + kumāra.

sources.title: Bhavanavāsi — definition (Jain class of residential gods)
sources.consultedFor: Confirming Nāgakumāra as one of the bhavanapati/bhavanavāsin classes whose realms hold the counted Jina-temples.

sources.title: Samyaktva — definition (Śvetāmbara & Digambara 'right faith'; Hemacandra's deva-guru-dharma formula)
sources.consultedFor: Glossing samakitavāsī and grounding the doctrinal link between caitya-vandana and the cultivation of samyaktva.

sources.title: Cologne Digital Sanskrit Dictionaries — Monier-Williams s.v. nāga-kumāra
sources.consultedFor: The lexical sense of nāgakumāra as a class of Bhavanādhīśa deities, supporting the per-token gloss and etymology.

notes: The verse omits the head-noun 'jinhar/jinālaya' after the number in line 1 (it is supplied from the parallel couplets and restated as jinhar in line 2); the bracketed 'Jina-temples' in the literal rendering reflects this ellipsis. The figure of eighty-four lakh śāśvata Jina-temples for the Nāgakumāra is a traditional Śvetāmbara cosmographic count (continuous with the Asurakumāra's 64 lakh in the preceding verse and Suvarṇakumāra's 72 lakh / Vāyukumāra's 96 lakh in the following one) and is preserved as a devotional figure, not independently audited. Spellings (jinhar, corāśī, māṁhe, samakitavāsī) are kept verbatim per the book's orthography; māṁhe and etā are Old-Gujarati/Apabhraṁśa forms. Precise online attestation of the exact 84-lakh tally for this specific class is limited, so the cosmographic detail is flagged as traditional rather than text-critically fixed.
